body_text:
疏忽 or Ču-wu 主兀. This form can have been transcribed only on the basis of
New Persian Juhūš or Jahūš with initial palatal sonant. As is well known, the
change of initial y into j is peculiar to New Persian.¹ In Pahlavi we have Yahūt,
as in Hebrew Yehūdī and in Arabic Yahūd. A Middle-Persian Yahūt would have
been very easy for the Chinese to transcribe. The very form of their transcription
shows, however, that it was modelled on the New-Persian type, and that it cannot
be much older than the tenth century or the age of the Sung.
footnotes: ¹ Cf. Horn, Grundr. iran. Phil., Vol. I, pt. 2, p. 73.
